ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Korean hot pot chicken (dak jeongol). FOODON_03302051 (chicken dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Korean chicken hot pot. A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible parent. The dish includes vegetables and broth components not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to Korean hot pot chicken as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT clinical food concepts do not cover this specific preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Korean hot pot chicken is approximately 400 g, comprising chicken pieces, vegetables, tofu, and broth served in a communal or individual hot pot vessel.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken stew / braised chicken with vegetables — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Korean hot pot chicken (dak jeongol) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for braised/stewed chicken with vegetables (NDB 05064, cooked chicken thigh meat; NDB 11529, mixed vegetables) and FAO/INFOODS SEA composite data for Korean-style chicken stew. Nutrient values represent a weighted composite for a dish of approximately 40% chicken, 20% vegetables, 10% tofu, and 30% broth. Sodium is elevated due to gochujang, soy sauce, and doenjang seasoning typical of this dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed chicken and vegetable stew — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for braised chicken dishes and mixed vegetable stews
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean hot pot chicken (dak jeongol). GI estimated at 45 based on analogous University of Sydney GI Database entries: braised chicken dishes (GI ~40–50) and mixed vegetable stews with moderate carbohydrate content. The dish contains gochujang and doenjang which contribute fermented carbohydrates; overall GI is moderated by the high protein and fat content of the chicken and the low-GI vegetable components. GL per 400 g serving calculated as (45 × 22.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 10.1. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from gochujang, doenjang, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a 400 g serving provides approximately 1,920 mg sodium — approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load with fermented carbohydrate seasonings
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low GI of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of ~10 per serving, Korean hot pot chicken is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without additional rice; however, the gochujang and doenjang pastes contribute fermentable sugars and sodium that warrant portion awareness.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from chicken thigh meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1,920 mg per serving) poses a cardiovascular risk for individuals with existing heart disease; choosing lean chicken breast over thigh and reducing fermented paste quantities can lower both sodium and saturated fat intake to more cardioprotective levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Pot Chicken (Korean: 핫팟 치킨; Chinese: 火锅鸡; also known as Dak Jeongol, 닭전골) is a Korean-style hot pot dish featuring bone-in or boneless chicken pieces simmered in a richly seasoned broth made with gochujang (Korean red chilli paste), doenjang (fermented soybean paste), garlic, ginger, and soy sauce. The dish is cooked and served in a communal hot pot vessel over a portable burner, with vegetables such as mushrooms, zucchini, spring onion, and cabbage, as well as tofu and glass noodles added to the simmering broth. The flavour profile is deeply savoury, mildly spicy, and umami-rich, with the fermented pastes providing complexity and depth. The texture of the chicken is tender and moist from slow simmering, while the vegetables retain a slight bite. In Singapore, this dish is served at Korean restaurants and is popular as a communal meal. Nutritionally, hot pot chicken is a moderate-energy dish providing approximately 112 kcal per 100 g, with a good protein content of ~10.8 g per 100 g from the chicken, and a relatively low carbohydrate content of ~5.6 g per 100 g. The primary clinical concern is the high sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g) from the fermented seasonings, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), making it broadly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without additional rice. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal when prepared with halal-certified chicken and sauces.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Korean hot pot chicken exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy braised chicken and mixed vegetable entries with a composite weighting approach. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and the analogous sources are approved primary sources.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA composite sources used for this estimation. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for braised chicken — naturally zero for this preparation as no partially hydrogenated oils are used. The food_name 'Hot Pot Chicken' is used as the canonical English name; the input record listed '火锅鸡 Hot Pot Chicken' which combines Chinese and English — the Chinese form is preserved in local_names and aliases. The cuisine tag 'Korean' is retained from the input; the dish is served at a Korean restaurant (Mincheng Bibimbap) in Singapore. Sodium values are elevated due to gochujang, doenjang, and soy sauce typical of this dish style. GI estimated at 45 (low, ≤55 threshold) from analogous University of Sydney GI Database entries for braised chicken stew dishes. GL per 400 g serving = (45 × 22.4) / 100 = 10.1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite data and are approximate. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable for this Korean dish and are left null per multilingual rules (Korean cuisine requires only Korean native-script name).
